subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: This guide is written for the teacher or parent who will lead a young child (age 5–6) through Unit 10. Year 1 learners are usually pre-readers, so this unit is taught with you, not read alone by the child. Your job is to hand the child real solid objects to hold, turn and sort, while you supply the words and the simple scripts in this guide. The child's own work happens in the companion Activity Sheet and Practice documents.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 1 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Name common 3D shapes: cube, cuboid, sphere, cylinder, cone and pyramid.
Explain that a 3D shape is solid and takes up space, and that a 2D shape is flat.
Point to and count the flat faces on a cube and a cuboid.
Describe a curved surface and say which shapes have one.
Sort shapes into groups such as rolls / does not roll and stacks / does not stack.
Match a 3D shape to an everyday object of the same shape.
Say what is the same and what is different between two shapes.
Make and continue a simple pattern using 3D shapes.
A cube: a dice, a stock cube, or a small square building block.

concepts.title: Solid (3D) shapes take up space
concepts.explanation: Start with the big idea: 3D shapes are solid and take up space, while 2D shapes are flat. The child does not need the words ‘dimension’ or ‘depth’ — they need to feel the difference. Put a flat paper circle on the table next to a real orange. Let the child hold both. The paper is flat and floppy; the orange is solid and round, and you can turn it in your hand. How to teach it: Gather four or five solid objects from around the home — a tin, a date box, a ball, a dice. Let the child pick each one up, turn it over,…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Hold the orange. Can you feel that it is solid all the way round?” Child: “Yes, it is round and hard.” You: “That makes it a 3D shape — it takes up space. Now feel this flat paper circle.…

concepts.title: Cubes and cuboids — faces and edges
concepts.explanation: A cube is a solid with 6 flat faces, and every face is a square the same size — think of a dice or a small building block. A cuboid also has 6 flat faces, but they are rectangles, so it looks like a stretched box — think of a date box, a matchbox, or a book. Both the cube and the cuboid have flat faces and straight edges, which is why they stack so well into towers. How to teach it: Give the child a dice (cube) and a date box (cuboid). Help them touch and count the faces — press one finger on each flat side,…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Let us count the flat faces on the dice. Touch each one as we turn it.” You and child: “One… two… three… four… five… six.” You: “Six faces! And they are all squares. That makes it a cube.”…

concepts.title: Spheres and cylinders — curved surfaces
concepts.explanation: A sphere is round all over, like a ball or an orange. It has one curved surface, no flat faces, and no edges — nothing flat to rest on — so it rolls in every direction. A cylinder is like a tin of tomatoes: it has two flat round ends (both circles, the same size) joined by one curved surface around the side. Because it has flat ends, a cylinder can stand and stack on its ends, but because its side is curved, it will also roll when it is on its side. How to teach it: Hand the child an orange and a tin. Run their…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Feel the orange. Can you find any flat part?” Child: “No, it is round everywhere.” You: “That curved surface is why it rolls. Now feel the tin — flat top, flat bottom, curved side.” You:…

concepts.title: Cones and pyramids — shapes with a point
concepts.explanation: A cone has one flat round face at the bottom and a curved surface that rises to a single point at the top — think of a party hat or an ice-cream cone. A pyramid has a flat base (often a square or a triangle) and flat triangle faces that lean in and meet at a point at the top — think of the great pyramids or a tent. Both shapes come to a point, which children love, and both can sit still on their flat bottom. How to teach it: A cone is easy to make — roll a semicircle of paper into a party-hat shape and tape it.…
concepts.example: You can gently explore that a cone is a little like a cylinder that has been squeezed to a point, and a pyramid is a little like a cuboid that has been squeezed to a point. This is just playful talk, not a rule to…

concepts.title: Sorting — rolls, stacks and everyday objects
concepts.explanation: This is where the unit comes together. Once the child can name the shapes, they should sort them by what they do. Two sorts work beautifully for this age. First, rolls / does not roll: spheres and cylinders and cones have curved surfaces, so they roll; cubes and cuboids have only flat faces, so they do not. Second, stacks / does not stack: shapes with flat faces (cube, cuboid, and a cylinder on its flat end) can be built into a tower, while a sphere cannot balance and will roll away. How to teach it: Draw two big…
concepts.example: Finish by linking every shape back to everyday objects, because this is what makes geometry stick. Walk around the home together on a ‘shape hunt’: the tin and the cup are cylinders, the orange and the football are…

concepts.title: Key idea 1
concepts.explanation: You can gently explore that a cone is a little like a cylinder that has been squeezed to a point, and a pyramid is a little like a cuboid that has been squeezed to a point. This is just playful talk, not a rule to memorise — but it helps the child see how the solid shapes relate to one another.
concepts.example: You can gently explore that a cone is a little like a cylinder that has been squeezed to a point, and a pyramid is a little like a cuboid that has been squeezed to a point. This is just playful talk, not a rule to memorise — but it helps the child see how the solid shapes relate to one another.

activities.title: Practise together
activities.materials: Paper, pencil and familiar household objects
steps: Choose one idea from this unit.
Build or draw a model of it.
Explain your model to a family member.
Write one question of your own and solve it.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: You can gently explore that a cone is a little like a cylinder that has been squeezed to a point, and a pyramid is a little like a cuboid that has been squeezed to a point. This is just playful talk, not a rule to memorise — but it helps the child see how the solid shapes relate to one another.

terms: A solid shape that takes up space; you can hold it
A flat shape, like a shape drawn on paper
A flat surface on a 3D shape
The line where two faces meet
To move by turning over and over
To build up, one on top of another
Round all over, one curved surface
2 round flat ends, curved in between
A round flat bottom rising to a point

questions.question: What does “3D shape” mean?
options: A solid shape that takes up space; you can hold it
A flat shape, like a shape drawn on paper
A flat surface on a 3D shape
The line where two faces meet
questions.answer: A solid shape that takes up space; you can hold it
questions.hint: Use the Unit 10 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: 3D shape means A solid shape that takes up space; you can hold it.

questions.question: What does “2D shape” mean?
options: A flat shape, like a shape drawn on paper
A flat surface on a 3D shape
The line where two faces meet
questions.answer: A flat shape, like a shape drawn on paper
questions.hint: Use the Unit 10 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: 2D shape means A flat shape, like a shape drawn on paper.

questions.question: What does “Face” mean?
options: A flat surface on a 3D shape
The line where two faces meet
To move by turning over and over
questions.answer: A flat surface on a 3D shape
questions.hint: Use the Unit 10 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Face means A flat surface on a 3D shape.

questions.question: What does “Edge” mean?
options: The line where two faces meet
To move by turning over and over
To build up, one on top of another
questions.answer: The line where two faces meet
questions.hint: Use the Unit 10 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Edge means The line where two faces meet.

questions.question: What does “Curved surface” mean?
options: To move by turning over and over
To build up, one on top of another
questions.hint: Use the Unit 10 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Curved surface means A rounded, not-flat surface.

questions.question: What does “Roll” mean?
options: To move by turning over and over
To build up, one on top of another
questions.answer: To move by turning over and over
questions.hint: Use the Unit 10 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Roll means To move by turning over and over.

questions.question: Which term matches this meaning: To build up, one on top of another?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 10 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Stack means To build up, one on top of another.

questions.question: Which term matches this meaning: 6 square faces, all the same?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 10 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Cube means 6 square faces, all the same.

questions.question: Which term matches this meaning: 6 flat faces shaped like rectangles?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 10 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Cuboid means 6 flat faces shaped like rectangles.

questions.question: Which term matches this meaning: Round all over, one curved surface?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 10 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Sphere means Round all over, one curved surface.

questions.question: Which term matches this meaning: 2 round flat ends, curved in between?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 10 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Cylinder means 2 round flat ends, curved in between.

questions.question: Which term matches this meaning: A round flat bottom rising to a point?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 10 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Cone means A round flat bottom rising to a point.

selfAssessment: I can name common 3D shapes: cube, cuboid, sphere, cylinder, cone and pyramid.
I can explain that a 3D shape is solid and takes up space, and that a 2D shape is flat.
I can point to and count the flat faces on a cube and a cuboid.
I can describe a curved surface and say which shapes have one.
I can sort shapes into groups such as rolls / does not roll and stacks / does not stack.
I can match a 3D shape to an everyday object of the same shape.
I can say what is the same and what is different between two shapes.
I can make and continue a simple pattern using 3D shapes.
